title: Method for fetal ultrasound image classification using pseudo-labelling with PCA-KMeans and an attention-augmented MobileNet-LSTM model
author: Shahade, Deshmukh, et al.
description: Accurate classification of fetal ultrasound images is critical for early diagnosis, yet remains challenging due to limited labeled data and high inter-class variability. This study presents a robust deep learning framework that combines a MobileNet backbone with multi-head self-attention and LSTM layers to enhance feature learning and temporal context. To address data scarcity and imbalance, unsupervised clustering was employed using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction and K-means (k=4) for pseudo-label generation. These pseudo-labeled clusters were then balanced using oversampling techniques. The proposed model was trained using transfer learning on the augmented dataset and achieved a test accuracy of approximately 98 % with a macro-F1 score of 0.98, indicating highly reliable classification performance.•Employed PCA (100 components) and K-means (k=4) for effective pseudo-labeling and class balancing.•Designed a hybrid deep learning architecture using MobileNet, multi-head attention, and LSTM.•Achieved ∼98 % test accuracy and 0.98 macro-F1 score, demonstrating strong model generalization.

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: 1. Accuracy and Loss curves while training:a. Accuracy Curve:

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Fig. 6 shows the accuracy curve. Validation starts high (nearly ∼97 percent) because frozen ImageNet MobileNet picks up useful features, while the training sits lower (∼93 percent). After this, only the attention + LSTM head learns, and the training accuracy achieves a high of 99 percent by epoch 4, leaving the validation to be near constant ∼98 percent. The consistent ∼1 percent gap and parallel trends signify learning with no over-fitting.b. Loss Curve:Fig. 6Accuracy curve.Fig. 6

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Fig. 7 shows the loss curve. The training loss decreases progressively from 0.17 to 0.03, validation loss slightly raises itself again after the first epoch when the new head settles down and then drops to an approximately value of 0.06, followed by a small raise at the tail-end. This indicates that improvements are harder to achieve, so it makes sense to stop early.Fig. 7Loss curve.Fig. 7

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Both curves show evidence of rapid convergence, a good alignment, without any apparent memorization and promising signs indicating a healthy training.2. The Final model showed the results ag given in the metric table:

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Clusters 0 & 1: Very high recall and precision (0.99 and ≥ 0.96) imply that the model almost never confuses these groups with others.

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Cluster 2: perfect marks from all angles: this visual group is extremely distinctive for the network.

steps_raw.title: Method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Cluster 3: Precision remains high at 0.99, whereas recall dropped to 0.94, meaning that ∼6 % of true-cluster-3 images are misclassified, generally as either cluster 0 or 1 (which can be seen in the confusion matrix). That minor shortcoming could be mitigated through focused data augmentation or re-weighting.
